facts.label: FASTEST OWN GOAL EVER
facts.story: Bosnia and Herzegovina's World Cup debut lasted all of two minutes and nine seconds before it went wrong. Lionel Messi's free kick deflected off Marcos Rojo and cannoned in off defender Sead Kolašinac for an own goal, breaking the record for fastest own goal in World Cup history — beating Carlos Gamarra's 2006 mark by 37 seconds. Kolašinac would go on to build a fine club career at Arsenal, but in Rio de Janeiro that June night, his name entered the record books for all the wrong reasons.

facts.label: FROM LILIES TO DRAGONS
facts.story: After independence in 1992, Bosnian fans nicknamed the new national team the 'Golden Lilies,' echoing the fleur-de-lis on the medieval Kotromanić coat of arms that once adorned the country's flag. As the team's identity evolved, the nickname shifted to 'Zmajevi' — the Dragons — a nod to Husein Gradaščević, the 19th-century general known as the 'Dragon of Bosnia' for leading an uprising against Ottoman rule. The name change tracked a nation redefining itself decades after its flag was redesigned in 1998.

2014.headline: Two Minutes, One Record

annotations.label: RECORD IN 129 SECONDS
annotations.story: Bosnia's World Cup debut was 129 seconds old when Messi's deflected free kick cannoned in off Sead Kolašinac for an own goal — the fastest in World Cup history. It was a jarring start to six decades of waiting for this moment, undone before most fans had found their seats.

annotations.label: IBIŠEVIĆ WRITES THE FIRST PAGE
annotations.story: Down 2–0 with five minutes left against Argentina, substitute Vedad Ibišević latched onto Senad Lulić's pass and finished left-footed under the goalkeeper — Bosnia and Herzegovina's first-ever World Cup goal. It didn't save the match, but it gave a new nation its opening line in the tournament's history book.

annotations.label: FIRST WIN, LAST HURRAH
annotations.story: Already eliminated, Bosnia closed its debut tournament in style: Edin Džeko and Miralem Pjanić scored either side of halftime, and substitute Avdija Vršajević added a third to beat Iran 3–1 in Salvador. It was Bosnia and Herzegovina's first win in World Cup history — arriving one match too late to matter, but the record books don't care about timing.

2014.homeColors: royal blue shirt with white Adidas shoulder stripes and trim
2014.awayColors: white shirt with blue Adidas shoulder stripes and trim
